title: Fault Suppression Policy Audit (Cisco UCS)
exclusions: This does not label a part replacement as a governance violation when your Cisco TAC RMA and maintenance plan legitimately use Cisco’s maintenance window object (maintenanceMaintWindow) or a fault suppression task under pol/fault-suppression-task during a documented, CAB-backed change: record those as approved in ucs_maintenance_windows.csv instead of as findings. It does not cover Cisco Intersight–only estates where the authoritative audit is not cisco:ucs:audit; use UC-19.1.23 for that control plane. It cannot show operator names for suppressions that age off before your audit retention on cisco:ucs:audit, even if fault objects remain visible, so align earliest= with a retention you can prove. It does not rate HyperFlex or application-only stories that never traverse these UCSM sourcetypes, and it is not a cross-domain identity control by itself; pair with your PAM evidence where required.
evidence: Saved report or alert `uc_19_1_12_fault_suppression_governance` (or your name) on `index=cisco_ucs` plus a weekly CSV in a WORM or locked object store with `code`, `dn`, `audit_users`, `max_occur`, and `severity_bucket` for the compliance owner.
dataSources: Primary index: cisco_ucs. Current hardware fault state: sourcetype cisco:ucs:faults (faultInst via the add-on’s configResolveClass poll to UCS Manager). Administrator accountability: sourcetype cisco:ucs:audit, carrying aaaModLR-style changes with user, affectedObject (join key to the fault distinguished name), and descr. Optional context from cisco:ucs:syslog and cisco:ucs:events does not drive the join but helps triage when audit descr text is short.
app: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), v4.2+ on Splunk Enterprise 9.1+ / 10.x and Splunk Cloud; install on a heavy forwarder for modular inputs and on search heads). The on-disk app id uses an underscore.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.1.12 — Fault Suppression Policy Audit (Cisco UCS): chronic suppressors, recurring fault codes, and ack/suppress behaviour by joining faultInst on cisco:ucs:faults with cisco:ucs:audit (aaaModLR) on the fault distinguished name; adjust thresholds, lookup rows, and time windows to your org.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-30d@d
| eval lc=lower(coalesce(lc, lifecycle, lifecycleState, Lc, ""))
| eval ack=lower(coalesce(ack, acknowledged, Ack, ""))
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, severityType, Severity, "")
| eval code=coalesce(code, Code, faultCode, "")
| eval cause=coalesce(cause, Cause, faultCause, "")
| eval occur=tonumber(coalesce(occur, Occur, "0"))
| where isnotnull(dn) AND match(dn,"^sys\/")
| where lc="suppressed" OR like(lc,"%suppress%") OR ack="yes" OR ack="y" OR like(ack,"y%")
| stats count as fault_event_count, values(severity) as severities, max(_time) as last_fault_time, max(occur) as max_occur, min(_time) as first_fault_time by code, dn, cause
| join type=left max=20000 dn [
    search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:audit" earliest=-30d@d
    | eval object_dn=trim(coalesce(affectedObject, AffectedObject, affected_object, affectedDn, Affected_Dn, ""))
    | where isnotnull(object_dn) AND match(object_dn, "^sys\/")
    | rename object_dn as dn
    | eval who=coalesce(user, User, affectedUser, affected_user)
    | eval adesc=coalesce(descr, Descr, description, msg, Message)
    | stats values(who) as audit_users, values(adesc) as audit_messages, min(_time) as first_audit_time, max(_time) as last_audit_time, count as audit_events by dn
  ]
| eval join_age_days=round((now()-last_fault_time)/86400,2)
| lookup ucs_maintenance_windows.csv code OUTPUT maint_state
| where isnull(maint_state) OR maint_state="" OR (maint_state!="approved_silence" AND maint_state!="freeze")
| eval business_service=case(match(dn,"/(?i)prod/"),"prod", match(dn,"/(?i)test/"),"test", match(dn,"/(?i)lab/"),"lab", match(dn,"/(?i)dr/"),"dr", true(),"other")
| where fault_event_count>=3
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    max_occur>=30 AND business_service="prod" AND (isnull(maint_state) OR maint_state=""),"critical",
    max_occur>=20 OR fault_event_count>=20,"high",
    fault_event_count>=10,"medium",
    1=1,"low"
  )
| table first_fault_time, last_fault_time, first_audit_time, last_audit_time, code, dn, cause, severities, fault_event_count, max_occur, join_age_days, audit_users, audit_messages, audit_events, business_service, severity_bucket, maint_state
| sort - max_occur, - fault_event_count
description: Detects chronic or recurring fault code and distinguished-name patterns on current Cisco UCS Manager fault objects where the lifecycle (lc) is suppressed and/or the fault is acknowledged, then enriches each row with the administrator identity and audit text from cisco:ucs:audit (aaaModLR-style modification log lines) matched on the affectedObject distinguished name so you can name who last acknowledged or applied suppression, not just the static fault state from faultInst.
value: The business and audit value is a defensible, repeatable who-did-what on recurring hardware failure signals: you catch teams that habitually silence the same F-series codes on production blades instead of completing DIMM, I/O, or firmware remediation, and you can show operator account names and UCS Manager distinguished names on FI-managed fabrics in a way a raw fault table alone cannot support for an internal or external control review.
implementation: Install the Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS, enable both faultInst (cisco:ucs:faults) and audit (cisco:ucs:audit) modular inputs into cisco_ucs, deploy ucs_maintenance_windows.csv for approved silence, save the use-case spl as a governance alert with 12–24h throttle on code and dn, and route results to the Cisco compute operations queue and SOAR owner.
visualization: (1) Bar of top audit_users. (2) Bar of top fault code. (3) Time series of governance findings per day. (4) Table with dn, code, cause, max_occur, audit_users, audit messages, business_service, severity_bucket. (5) Optional drill to cisco:ucs:events for the same dn.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t count as change_n from datamodel=Change
  where nodename=Change.All_Changes
  by All_Changes.user All_Changes.object
| head 20
| append [
    | tstats summariesonly=t count as inv_n from datamodel=Inventory
      where nodename=Inventory.Computer_Inventory
      by host
| head 20 ]
dataModelAcceleration: Keep the active alert on raw cisco:ucs:faults and cisco:ucs:audit until peer-reviewed field aliases and tags map audit and fault rows into the Change and Inventory data models. Accelerate those models only after a CIM validation pass; the cimSpl is then a faster review aid, not a substitute for the native UCSM join in early deployment.

references.title: Cisco UCS Manager XML API — faultInst and system classes (release-specific)

knownFalsePositives: Genuine but benign patterns: a Cisco TAC-authorized RMA where pol/fault-suppression-task is used for the RMA window; a UCSM firmware or infrastructure bundle with a CAB slot tied to maintenanceMaintWindow while a DIMM or I/O code is acked only until parts arrive; a DR or business-continuity exercise with bulk ack of non-production FIs; a TAC data collection where an engineer suppresses faults under an explicit case ID your Cisco UCS operations lead can reference in the ticket system. The distinction from UC-19.1.14 (UCSM backup and mgmtBackup age) and UC-19.1.23 (Intersight aaa audit records) is that the noise here is usually an approved change window, so your defence is a maintained lookup and governance review, not only raising numeric thresholds.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

• Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs (Splunkbase 2731) on a heavy forwarder and on search heads for scheduled searches, lookups, and alerts. • A dedicated index (for example cisco_ucs) with least-privilege roles for alert-runners, not the admin role. • Outbound HTTPS 443 to the UCS Manager cluster virtual IP, trusted TLS, and a read-only service account for fault and aaa audit class reads only. • A named runbook and SOAR owner (for example Cisco UCS Operations Lead) so results route to a compute governance queue, not a generic NOC. • Optional: lookups/ucs_maintenance_windows.csv in a companion app, with transforms.conf and read permissions, to align the alert with your CAB-reported maintenance, RMA, and firmware work.



The first time you read this use case, note four anchors: the **faultInst** object lands in cisco:ucs:faults; the administrator who acked or suppressed is not reliable from faultInst alone; the **aaaModLR**-style log appears as cisco:ucs:audit with affectedObject; and **Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs** is the supported path to poll both.



**Step 1 — Configure data collection**

Enable both cisco:ucs:faults and cisco:ucs:audit on the same forwarder. In local inputs.conf in Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs, locate the
ciscoucs (or equivalent) stanzas your build ships, and reconcile every key to default/inputs.conf. Do not paste fantasy stanza
names. Set index=cisco_ucs, set fault poll for faultInst so classId or stats_classes includes faultInst, and set audit so the
parallel poll for aaa or modification logs resolves to cisco:ucs:audit. Typical healthy intervals: 60 to 300 seconds for each,
with audit no slower than the fault interval if you need tight time alignment. After restart, use Settings → Data → Sourcetypes
to confirm both sourcetype names appear with recent data.

props.conf: for [cisco:ucs:faults] and [cisco:ucs:audit], set SHOULD_LINEMERGE=false, TRUNCATE high enough for large MO rows,
KV_MODE=json, LINE_BREAKER to newline, and time extraction only after a sample of _raw from your UCSM build is in hand, because
Cisco minor releases sometimes shift the timestamp field. Export props on indexers and search heads as your pipeline requires.

RBAC: create a read-only role that can resolve faultInst and audit MOs but cannot change fault suppression or service profiles.
Cisco’s aaa-monitor and read-only patterns achieve this intent; the exact name depends on your UCSM release. Service accounts
that can ack faults break the control story, so avoid them for polling.

Volume planning: a busy dual-FI domain can emit a handful of fault events per day when healthy, but thousands of audit rows
because not every line is a fault. Budget index volume accordingly and set retention to cover your ITGC or internal audit
lookback, often 13 months online for quarterly evidence.

For ucs_maintenance_windows.csv, a minimal file is:
code,maint_state,justification,change_id
F0123,approved_silence,example FI firmware change,CHG0001
Use maint_state values approved_silence or freeze only for CAB records you trust. The primary spl treats empty or absent rows as
not approved. The Cisco maintenanceMaintWindow and pol/fault-suppression-task mechanisms are the UCSM side of the same story;
your lookup is the human governance wrapper so authorized silence does not page as a defect.

Closing notes for evidence hygiene: some teams schedule a weekly report that writes a redacted CSV of the dashboard table to a
locked share; match that cadence to your own ITGC sampling window. Build a one-page field matrix you update when Cisco ships a
new Splunk add-on build: for faults, test dn, code, lc, ack, occur, and lastTransition; for audit, test affectedObject, user, descr.
If the add-on renames a column to avoid collision with Splunk internal fields, change your local saved-search coalesce in one
place and re-run the validation path in Step 3.

UCSM RBAC: bind the read-only poller to a custom role that includes read-only service profile, equipment, fault, and AAA audit
privileges. Cisco often documents an aaa-monitoring or read-only pair; the permission label aaa-monitor-priv is the one many
4.x runbooks use for log-style AAA reads without config-write rights. The account you pair with ciscoucs in inputs.conf should map
to that kind of role, not to admin, not to operations-ack, and not to a personal user credential.

The inputs.conf example below is structural: stanza names, boolean flags, and interval keys differ between Splunk add-on 4.2, 4.3,
and hotfix branches. You must line up every key to Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs default/inputs.conf in your exact package before you restart
splunkd; the intent of the sample is to show that fault and audit polling are separate toggles and separate intervals you can
keep in the same 120 s band for this governance story.

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf
# Shape-only example — copy the real stanza name, account= handle, and every
# fault/audit/class/toggle key from Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/default/inputs.conf for
# your exact 2731 build; do not invent boolean names the modular input does not list.

[ciscoucs://example_ucsm_domain]
account = svc_ucs_readonly_poll
disabled = 0
index = cisco_ucs
interval = 120
# After you merge vendor defaults into this stanza, you must have:
# (1) A poll that resolves faultInst (or equivalent) into sourcetype cisco:ucs:faults.
# (2) A poll that resolves AAA modification / session logs (aaaModLR family) into
#     sourcetype cisco:ucs:audit.
# Use the vendor's separate interval= lines if the TA exposes fault_interval and
# audit_interval; if the TA uses a single interval, set it to the tighter of the
# two governance needs (usually the fault side) and verify audit still keeps up.
# Optional: disable stats, inventory, or event MOs you do not need to cap API load.
```

If your build names the booleans `enable_*` or places audit under a combined event stream, follow the vendor file — this UC
needs both cisco:ucs:faults and cisco:ucs:audit, whatever keys achieve that in your build after upgrade.

transforms.conf for the optional maintenance list:

```
[ucs_maintenance_windows]
filename = ucs_maintenance_windows.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```

Settings → Lookups → Lookup table files: grant read to the same role that schedules the alert, not only admin.




**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**

Run the same SPL in Search as a report until counts match what you can verify in the UCSM UI. The pipeline: narrow current faults to suppressed or acked states, aggregate to measure chronicity, left join cisco:ucs:audit on the fault dn from affectedObject, apply a maintenance lookup, compute business_service and severity_bucket, and sort. For heavy estates, if join CPU is an issue, materialise a 15 minute stats of audit by affectedObject to a summary index and swap join for a lookup. Schedule the governance alert on a 15 to 30 minute boundary with throttle on code and dn for 12 to 24 hours. Route critical and high rows to the Cisco UCS operations lead and SOAR queue, not a generic bridge.



Search and alert (paste exactly; then save as report or alert):



```spl

`comment("UC-19.1.12 — Fault Suppression Policy Audit (Cisco UCS): chronic suppressors, recurring fault codes, and ack/suppress behaviour by joining faultInst on cisco:ucs:faults with cisco:ucs:audit (aaaModLR) on the fault distinguished name; adjust thresholds, lookup rows, and time windows to your org.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-30d@d
| eval lc=lower(coalesce(lc, lifecycle, lifecycleState, Lc, ""))
| eval ack=lower(coalesce(ack, acknowledged, Ack, ""))
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, severityType, Severity, "")
| eval code=coalesce(code, Code, faultCode, "")
| eval cause=coalesce(cause, Cause, faultCause, "")
| eval occur=tonumber(coalesce(occur, Occur, "0"))
| where isnotnull(dn) AND match(dn,"^sys\/")
| where lc="suppressed" OR like(lc,"%suppress%") OR ack="yes" OR ack="y" OR like(ack,"y%")
| stats count as fault_event_count, values(severity) as severities, max(_time) as last_fault_time, max(occur) as max_occur, min(_time) as first_fault_time by code, dn, cause
| join type=left max=20000 dn [
    search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:audit" earliest=-30d@d
    | eval object_dn=trim(coalesce(affectedObject, AffectedObject, affected_object, affectedDn, Affected_Dn, ""))
    | where isnotnull(object_dn) AND match(object_dn, "^sys\/")
    | rename object_dn as dn
    | eval who=coalesce(user, User, affectedUser, affected_user)
    | eval adesc=coalesce(descr, Descr, description, msg, Message)
    | stats values(who) as audit_users, values(adesc) as audit_messages, min(_time) as first_audit_time, max(_time) as last_audit_time, count as audit_events by dn
  ]
| eval join_age_days=round((now()-last_fault_time)/86400,2)
| lookup ucs_maintenance_windows.csv code OUTPUT maint_state
| where isnull(maint_state) OR maint_state="" OR (maint_state!="approved_silence" AND maint_state!="freeze")
| eval business_service=case(match(dn,"/(?i)prod/"),"prod", match(dn,"/(?i)test/"),"test", match(dn,"/(?i)lab/"),"lab", match(dn,"/(?i)dr/"),"dr", true(),"other")
| where fault_event_count>=3
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    max_occur>=30 AND business_service="prod" AND (isnull(maint_state) OR maint_state=""),"critical",
    max_occur>=20 OR fault_event_count>=20,"high",
    fault_event_count>=10,"medium",
    1=1,"low"
  )
| table first_fault_time, last_fault_time, first_audit_time, last_audit_time, code, dn, cause, severities, fault_event_count, max_occur, join_age_days, audit_users, audit_messages, audit_events, business_service, severity_bucket, maint_state
| sort - max_occur, - fault_event_count

```



**Step 3 — Validate**

In UCSM, open Admin → Faults and find the same code and distinguished name the Splunk row shows. The suppressed or ack
state on screen should match the filters you used, allowing for one poll of skew.

Open Admin → User Activities → Audit Logs and locate a line where the affected object equals the fault distinguished name. The
account on that line should match the audit_users field in Splunk after the join, whether local, TACACS, or a mapped display name.
If you manage X-series only through Intersight and cisco:ucs:audit is not the control plane you trust, do not use this UC for that
estate: follow UC-19.1.23 for Intersight audit records. Compare last_fault_time in Splunk to the fault created/last transition
fields: large drift usually means NTP or time parsing issues on the forwarder or in props.

Prove your lookup: run | inputlookup ucs_maintenance_windows in the Search app and see your rows. If the lookup is missing, the
where clause should still return rows, only without maintenance exclusion.



**Step 4 — Operationalize**

Dashboard: row 1, horizontal bar of top 15 audit_users. Row 2, top fault code frequency for suppressed/acked chronic patterns.
Row 3, daily time series of qualifying rows, split by business_service if the dn heuristic matches your naming. Row 4, the audit
table: dn, code, cause, max_occur, audit_users, first and last audit times, a short audit message, and a column for a change ID
from your change system that you paste manually or join from a service desk export.

Run a weekly 15 minute governance huddle: review the dashboard, attach CAB numbers to any approved silence, and open
hardware remediation for upward trending max_occur. Quarterly, export a CSV to WORM or object-locked storage with the spl text,
version, and time range used. Name Cisco UCS operations lead (or your equivalent) as the accountable owner, not a generic team.



**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**

If the search returns no rows, verify both sourcetype= metadata on index=cisco_ucs, confirm ciscoucs stanzas are not disabled, and
test the UCSM VIP on 443 from the forwarder. If faults appear but audit_users is null, the audit path is the gap: re-enable
aaa or audit class polling, check sourcetype, and look for a field alias conflict on user if Splunk rewrites it.

If the search is too noisy, raise the fault_event_count and max_occur minimums, tighten business_service, and add rows to
ucs_maintenance_windows for the CAB-backed weekends you already know about.

If the user field is empty on audit, check TACACS to local mapping, and whether your TA used affectedUser. Extend the coalesce
list in a local version of the saved search, or set FIELDALIAS in props after a peer review, then re-test. If the join is slow,
reduce the lookback, switch to a summary of audit by object_dn, or raise subsearch limits with platform support sign-off, not
silently on production without change control.

If you must prove clearance timing for faults that are suppressed longer than a stated policy, add a separate saved search
that uses lastTransition on fault when present; this use case is intentionally about chronicity and who, not about every
possible clearance workflow.

Deeper design notes: Fabric Interconnect models such as FI-6332-16UP, FI-64108, and earlier 62xx/63xx generations do not change
the structure of a blade-level distinguished name, but an asymmetric path to the UCSM VIP can cause half the fault stream to lag.
If evidence looks one-sided, test TLS from the heavy forwarder to each management IP, not only the virtual hostname.

FibreChannel and Ethernet-adjacent faults often change severity more often than a slow-drift DIMM code. The default severity_bucket
case in the primary spl is a template; most mature shops replace that block with a lookup of fault code to a governance tier that
matches their Cisco TAC entitlements and internal severity matrix.

Service profile and org membership are not required to join, but a secondary lookup on a service profile token embedded in a dn
pattern, when your naming standard allows, helps app owners see why a fault matters beyond the chassis serial.

When you upgrade UCSM, run a one-week shadow mode on the new build in lab: compare fieldsummary on cisco:ucs:audit to your
production build and adjust coalesce only when a net-new spelling appears. Keep your alert description pointing at a Confluence
or internal wiki that lists your approved suppression policies and a contact for emergency bulk ack, so an auditor can see
process, not just numbers.

The business_service case statement is a dn substring heuristic. Replace that eval with a lookup of dn patterns to a business
tag after you have enough rows to know how your orgs are laid out. That keeps the same spl skeleton while letting governance own
the business mapping without a developer each time a new org code appears.
grandmaExplanation: We look for the same hardware trouble code being hushed or marked as handled over and over, and we match those server entries to a written record of which administrator did it, so a reviewer can see the whole story. We also skip the rows you have already tied to a planned maintenance pass so normal approved work is not called a surprise every week.
hardware: Cisco UCS Manager–managed B-series 5108 and UCSX-9508 X-series chassis, C220/C240 C-series in UCSM service-profile mode, and Fabric Interconnects such as FI-6332, FI-64108, and related 64xx/65xx families that host the management virtual IP used for HTTPS polls of faultInst and aaaModLR. Representative blades include B200 M5/M6, X210c M7 compute nodes, and C220 M6 rack servers where UCSM is the system of record for fault state. The use case is not a substitute for standalone CIMC-only C-series that never share the same sys/ distinguished-name tree, and it assumes the Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (Splunkbase 2731) can reach both fabric interconnects for polling.
